section.title: The Twelve Memorial Stones
section.sectionRationale: The miracle at the Jordan is not to be forgotten. God commands that twelve men take twelve stones from the middle of the riverbed to build a memorial, so that future generations will ask what they mean and be told the story of what God did. The nation completes the crossing, Joshua is exalted before all Israel, and the stones are set up at Gilgal. It is one movement, the deliberate making of a memory that will preach God's power to children not yet born.
section.thesis: God cares that His mighty acts be remembered and retold, so He turns the crossing into a lasting sign: stones that will prompt a child's question and a parent's testimony, teaching every generation that the LORD's hand is mighty and calling all the earth, and all Israel, to fear Him.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the command to take twelve stones, verses 1 to 8; the completed crossing and Joshua's exaltation, verses 9 to 14; the priests come up and the river returns, verses 15 to 18; and the stones set up at Gilgal to teach the generations, verses 19 to 24.

units.label: The command to take twelve stones
units.note: when your children ask, what do these stones mean

verses.text: When the whole nation had finished crossing the Jordan, the LORD said to Joshua,
“Choose twelve men from among the people, one from each tribe,
and command them: ‘Take up for yourselves twelve stones from the middle of the Jordan where the priests were standing, carry them with you, and set them down in the place where you spend the night.’”
So Joshua summoned the twelve men he had appointed from the Israelites, one from each tribe,
and said to them, “Cross over before the ark of the LORD your God into the middle of the Jordan. Each of you is to take a stone upon his shoulder, according to the number of the tribes of Israel,
to serve as a sign among you. In the future, when your children ask, ‘What do these stones mean to you?’

you are to tell them, ‘The waters of the Jordan were cut off before the ark of the covenant of the LORD. When it crossed the Jordan, the waters were cut off.’ Therefore these stones will be a {{zikkaron|memorial}} to the Israelites forever.”

Thus the Israelites did as Joshua had commanded them. They took up twelve stones from the middle of the Jordan, one for each tribe of Israel, just as the LORD had told Joshua; and they carried them to the camp, where they set them down.

units.label: The completed crossing and Joshua's exaltation
units.note: the LORD exalted Joshua in the sight of all Israel

verses.text: Joshua also set up twelve stones in the middle of the Jordan, in the place where the priests who carried the ark of the covenant stood. And the stones are there to this day.

Now the priests who carried the ark remained standing in the middle of the Jordan until the people had completed everything the LORD had commanded Joshua to tell them, just as Moses had directed Joshua. The people hurried across,
and after everyone had finished crossing, the priests with the ark of the LORD crossed in the sight of the people.
The Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh crossed over before the Israelites, armed for battle as Moses had instructed them.
About 40,000 troops armed for battle crossed over before the LORD into the plains of Jericho.
On that day the LORD exalted Joshua in the sight of all Israel, and they revered him all the days of his life, just as they had revered Moses.

units.label: The priests come up and the river returns
units.note: the waters of the Jordan returned to their course

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Joshua,
“Command the priests who carry the ark of the Testimony to come up from the Jordan.”
So Joshua commanded the priests, “Come up from the Jordan.”

When the priests carrying the ark of the covenant of the LORD came up out of the Jordan and their feet touched the dry land, the waters of the Jordan returned to their course and overflowed all the banks as before.

units.label: The stones at Gilgal to teach the generations
units.note: so that all the peoples of the earth may know the hand of the LORD is mighty

verses.text: On the tenth day of the first month the people went up from the Jordan and camped at Gilgal on the eastern border of Jericho.
And there at Gilgal Joshua set up the twelve stones they had taken from the Jordan.
Then Joshua said to the Israelites, “In the future, when your children ask their fathers, ‘What is the meaning of these stones?’
you are to tell them, ‘Israel crossed the Jordan on dry ground.’
For the LORD your God dried up the waters of the Jordan before you until you had crossed over, just as He did to the Red Sea, which He dried up before us until we had crossed over.
He did this so that all the peoples of the earth may know that the {{yad|hand}} of the LORD is mighty, and so that you may always {{yare|fear}} the LORD your God.”

zikkaron.gloss: A memorial, remembrance, reminder. The twelve stones are a zikkaron, a permanent prompt to memory built into the landscape, so that God's saving act at the Jordan will not be forgotten but retold to every generation. The same idea shapes the Passover and, ultimately, the Lord's Supper, given so that His people would remember and proclaim what God has done.

yad.gloss: Hand, power, might. The purpose of the memorial is that all the peoples of the earth may know the yad of the LORD is mighty. The hand stands for God's active power; the crossing displays it, and the stones preserve the testimony to it, so that both the nations and Israel would reckon with the strong hand of God.

yare.gloss: To fear, reverence, stand in awe. The final aim of the whole sign is that Israel may always yare the LORD their God, a reverent awe that leads to trust and obedience. The remembering is not for its own sake; it is to keep alive the fear of the LORD, the fitting response to the God whose mighty hand they had just seen.

basic.context: As soon as the whole nation has crossed the Jordan, God gives an unusual command: build a memorial. Twelve men, one per tribe, are to carry twelve stones from the middle of the dried riverbed and set them up as a permanent marker. The point is memory. One day children will ask, what do these stones mean, and their parents will tell the story of how God stopped the river so Israel could cross. Joshua also sets up stones in the riverbed itself. The priests come up, the water returns, and the stones are finally erected at Gilgal. God says the purpose is that all the earth would know His mighty hand and that Israel would always fear Him. The question underneath is: how does faith get passed from one generation to the next?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, and its clear teaching point is spelled out in the text itself: God's acts must be remembered and retold. The specific memorial of stones belongs to Israel's history, but the principle, deliberately building reminders and teaching children through them, is a lasting pattern God cares about deeply. The chapter also ties the Jordan crossing back to the Red Sea, reinforcing that this is a second exodus.
basic.originalLanguages: The stones are a zikkaron, a memorial, a built-in prompt to remembrance. Their purpose is that all the earth would know the yad, the hand, the mighty power of the LORD, and that Israel would always yare, fear, stand in awe of, their God.
basic.typology: The memorial stones that call God's people to remember His saving act point forward to the memorials God gives the church, above all the Lord's Supper, given so that we would remember and proclaim the greater deliverance won at the cross. The crossing they commemorate, a passage from wilderness to inheritance, pictures salvation in Christ.
basic.christ: The stones preserve the memory of a rescue through the waters, pointing to the far greater rescue accomplished by Jesus. As Israel was told to remember and retell the crossing, so Christ gives His people the bread and cup and says, do this in remembrance of Me, so that the mighty saving hand of God at the cross would be proclaimed until He comes.
basic.god: God is the God who acts mightily and who wants His acts known. He dried up the Jordan as He dried up the Red Sea, showing His unchanging power across the generations. He works so that all the peoples of the earth would know His hand, and so that His people would live in reverent fear of Him. He is a God concerned not only to save but to be remembered and worshiped.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 12 gives the Passover as a memorial to be explained to children. Psalm 78 urges telling the coming generation the praises of the LORD. Deuteronomy 6 commands teaching God's works to one's children. And 1 Corinthians 11 records the Lord's Supper as the church's memorial of Christ.
basic.oneStory: God turns the crossing into a lasting memory so His mighty acts will be retold forever. The twelve stones join the long line of God-given reminders, from Passover to the Lord's Supper, by which each generation learns what God has done. They point to the greater deliverance in Christ, which His people are likewise called to remember and proclaim.

context.literary: Chapter 4 completes the crossing begun in chapter 3 and interprets it through the memorial stones. The narrative moves in a slightly overlapping, layered way, characteristic of Hebrew storytelling, circling back to reinforce the key event and its meaning. Twice the child's question and the parent's answer are given, verses 6 to 7 and 21 to 23, framing the whole chapter around teaching the next generation. The closing verses tie the Jordan explicitly to the Red Sea and state the double purpose plainly.
context.historical: Gilgal, where the stones were set up, became Israel's base camp for the conquest, just inside the land on the plains near Jericho. The crossing took place on the tenth day of the first month, the very day the Passover lambs were to be selected, Exodus 12:3, linking the entry into the land to the exodus deliverance. Standing stones were a recognized way in the ancient world to mark a covenant, a boundary, or a memorable event; here they serve as a teaching monument. The repeated phrase to this day suggests the stones were still known to the book's first readers.
context.canonical: The memorial theme runs through Scripture: the Passover, the stones here, later altars and monuments, and finally the Lord's Supper, all given so that God's saving acts would be remembered and retold. The chapter's insistence on teaching children joins the strong biblical emphasis on passing faith down the generations, Deuteronomy 6 and Psalm 78. By binding the Jordan to the Red Sea, it frames Israel's whole journey as bracketed by God's power over the waters, and it anticipates the New Testament's greater exodus in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the making of a permanent memorial to the Jordan crossing, to show God confirming Joshua before all Israel, and to teach that God's mighty acts are to be remembered and retold to every generation so that all the earth may know His power and His people may fear Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific monument of twelve stones at Gilgal belongs to Israel's history and is not a ritual for the church to reproduce. What is prescriptive, and stated in the text itself, is the enduring duty and delight of remembering God's works and teaching them to our children, a pattern God repeats in the memorials He gives His people, culminating in the Lord's Supper.

debates.question: Were there two sets of stones, one at Gilgal and one left in the riverbed?

views.label: Two distinct memorials
views.case: The plain reading of verses 8 and 20 has twelve stones carried out and set up at Gilgal, while verse 9 says Joshua also set up twelve stones in the middle of the Jordan where the priests stood; two memorials, one visible on land and one marking the exact spot of the miracle, each testifying to the same event.

views.label: One memorial, with verse 9 clarified by the footnote
views.case: Some translators render verse 9 as Joshua set up the twelve stones that had been in the middle of the Jordan, as the BSB footnote notes, understanding a single set of stones taken from the river and erected at Gilgal, so the chapter describes one memorial from different angles.

debates.bearing: Either way the point stands: the crossing is deliberately marked with stone so that its memory endures; the number twelve, one per tribe, binds the whole nation to the testimony.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's vocabulary is the vocabulary of memory and awe. The twelve stones are a zikkaron, a memorial, a physical prompt built into the land so that a saving act of God would never slip out of Israel's memory but be told and retold; the same word and instinct lie behind the Passover, Exodus 12:14, and reach forward to the Lord's Supper, given so that Christ's people would remember and proclaim His death, 1 Corinthians 11:24-26. The stated purpose of the memorial is twofold. First, that all the peoples of the earth may know that the yad of the LORD is mighty: the hand is the standard biblical image of God's active, saving power, the same hand that struck Egypt and split the sea, now known again at the Jordan. Second, that Israel may always yare the LORD their God, that they may fear Him, live in reverent awe that bends into trust and obedience. Memorial, mighty hand, holy fear: the stones exist so that remembering God's power would keep alive the fear of the LORD in every generation.

typology.type: The memorial stones that preserve and retell a saving act
typology.fulfillment: The Lord's Supper, the memorial Christ gives His church so it would remember and proclaim His death until He comes, 1 Corinthians 11:24-26.

typology.type: The crossing of the waters into the inheritance, remembered forever
typology.fulfillment: The greater exodus accomplished by Christ, His people brought through death into life and the promised rest, Luke 9:31 and 1 Corinthians 10:1-4.

typology.type: The child's question answered by the story of God's deliverance
typology.fulfillment: The gospel handed down from generation to generation, the faith once for all delivered to the saints, 2 Timothy 1:5 and Jude 3.

study.christ: These stones exist so that a rescue through the waters would be remembered and retold forever, and in that they point straight to the greater rescue and the greater memorial. As God brought Israel through the Jordan and told them to mark it in stone and explain it to their children, so the Lord Jesus brings His people through death into life and gives them a memorial of His own: on the night He was betrayed He took bread and the cup and said, do this in remembrance of Me, 1 Corinthians 11:24. The child's question at the stones, what do these mean, is answered with the story of what God did; the church's remembrance at the table proclaims the Lord's death until He comes. The mighty hand of the LORD that dried up the Jordan is displayed most fully at the cross and the empty tomb, where God's power saved His people forever, and the whole earth is called to know it and to fear Him.
study.god: The God of this chapter is mighty and unchanging, and He wills to be remembered. He dried up the Jordan just as He had dried up the Red Sea a generation before, showing that the God who saved the fathers is the same God saving the children; His power does not fade with time. He acts on a stage as wide as the world, so that all the peoples of the earth may know that His hand is mighty, and He acts for the good of His people, that they may always fear Him, that reverent awe would anchor their trust and obedience. He is a God who cares not only that His people be saved but that His saving acts be told, treasured, and passed on, so that worship of Him would continue from generation to generation.

crossReferences.note: The Passover is given as a memorial to be explained to children when they ask its meaning, the same pattern as the stones.
When your son asks the meaning of God's commands, tell him the story of redemption; faith is handed down by testimony.
Tell the coming generation the praises and works of the LORD, that they would set their hope in God.
The Lord's Supper, the church's memorial, given so that Christ's saving death would be remembered and proclaimed until He comes.
The Jordan crossing is bound explicitly to the Red Sea, framing Israel's journey between two partings of the waters by God's hand.

study.oneStory: God turns the crossing of the Jordan into a lasting memory, twelve stones that will draw a child's question and a parent's testimony for generations to come. The memorial joins the long line of God-given reminders, from Passover to the Lord's Supper, by which each generation learns and proclaims what God has done. Binding the Jordan to the Red Sea, it shows the one saving God at work across the ages, and it points to the greater deliverance in Christ, which His people are likewise called to remember, retell, and proclaim until He returns.

quiz.q: Why did God command Israel to set up the twelve stones?

options.text: As a memorial, so that future generations would ask their meaning and be told what God had done.
options.note: Verses 6 to 7 and 21 to 23 make teaching the next generation the stated purpose.

options.text: As an altar for offering sacrifices to the LORD.
options.note: Stones were sometimes used for altars, but these were specifically a memorial to prompt remembrance, not an altar.

options.text: As a boundary marker for the tribe of Judah's land.
options.note: They marked no tribal boundary; they were a national memorial of the crossing.

options.text: As a defensive wall against Jericho.
options.note: They had no military purpose; they were a teaching memorial.

quiz.q: How many stones were taken, and why that number?

options.text: Twelve, one for each tribe of Israel, so the whole nation shared in the testimony.
options.note: Verses 2 to 8 tie the twelve stones to the twelve tribes, binding all Israel to the memory.

options.text: Twelve, one for each of Joshua's chief officers.
options.note: The number twelve is right, but it represents the tribes of Israel, not Joshua's officers.

options.text: Seven, matching the seven nations of Canaan.
options.note: There were twelve stones for the tribes, not seven for the nations.

options.text: A random pile with no particular number.
options.note: The number was deliberate: twelve, one per tribe.

quiz.q: What twofold purpose did God give for the whole event?

options.text: That all the earth would know His hand is mighty, and that Israel would always fear Him.
That Israel would grow wealthy and that their enemies would flee.
options.note: God did give victory, but the stated purpose was knowledge of His might and the fear of Him, not wealth.

options.text: That Joshua would be crowned king over Israel.
options.note: Joshua was exalted as leader, but the purpose given is God's glory and Israel's reverence, not a coronation.

options.text: That the memory of Moses would be erased.
options.note: Israel revered Joshua as they had revered Moses; nothing here erases Moses.

quiz.q: How does the memorial of stones point forward in Scripture?

options.text: To the memorials God gives His people to remember His saving acts, above all the Lord's Supper.
options.note: Remembering and retelling God's deliverance runs from Passover to the Lord's Supper, 1 Corinthians 11:24-26.

options.text: To the idea that God's works are best forgotten over time.
options.note: The opposite: the whole point is that God's works be remembered and retold, not forgotten.

options.text: To a command that every generation build its own stone pile.
options.note: The lasting principle is remembrance and teaching, not reproducing this specific monument.

options.text: To nothing beyond ancient Israel's history.
options.note: The pattern of God-given memorials continues to the church's remembrance of Christ.

questions: God built a reminder so His acts would not be forgotten. What practices or markers help you remember what God has done in your life, and where might you need more of them?
The stones existed to answer a child's question with the story of God's power. How are you passing on the story of what God has done to those coming after you?
God dried up the Jordan just as He had the Red Sea, showing His power is the same across generations. Where do you need to trust that the God who acted before will act again?
The purpose of remembering was that Israel would always fear the LORD. How does recalling God's mighty acts stir up reverent awe and trust in you today?
The Lord's Supper is the church's memorial of a greater crossing, the deliverance won at the cross. How can you come to it, or to any remembrance of Christ, with fresh wonder at God's saving hand?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes that some translators render this as Joshua set up the twelve stones that had been in the middle of the Jordan, which bears on whether there were one or two sets of stones.
The ark of the Testimony is another name for the ark of the covenant, as the BSB footnote notes; the Testimony refers to the covenant tablets it contained.
The tenth day of the first month is the day the Passover lamb was to be chosen, Exodus 12:3, linking the entry into the land to the exodus deliverance.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 4:1-24. Prose narrative. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none anywhere in the file. The number 40,000 in verse 13 is retained as in the BSB. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
